title: Symlink resolution loop has no iteration bound, risking infinite loop on circular symlinks
reasoning: The while loop follows symlinks by repeatedly calling readlink and re-checking -L. Unlike OS-level pathname resolution (which returns ELOOP after ~40 levels), readlink itself does not detect cycles — it simply reads the next target. A circular symlink chain (A→B→A) would cause this loop to spin indefinitely. In practice this is extremely unlikely for a package bin script, but a simple iteration counter (e.g., max 20) would make the script robust against misconfigured environments without meaningful complexity.
reproduction: ln -s prism.sh link_a && ln -s link_a prism_link && ./prism_link  # infinite loop (do not actually run)
recommendation: Add an iteration counter that breaks with an error after a reasonable limit (e.g., 20): `local i=0; while [ -L "$SOURCE" ]; do (( i++ > 20 )) && echo 'Too many symlink levels' >&2 && exit 1; ...; done`
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No tests are included for this feature, and testing infinite-loop guards requires constructing circular symlinks in a test fixture, which is non-trivial.
suggestedRegressionTest: Create a temporary directory with two symlinks pointing at each other, invoke the resolution logic (extracted into a testable function), and assert it exits with an error within a timeout rather than hanging.
minimumFixScope: Add a counter variable and a guard clause inside the while loop (3 lines).
